review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the indirection overhead introduced by MRU cluster caching in the virtual swap space, specifically on allocation and lookup operations. They acknowledge that this adds complexity but also improves performance. The author plans to address these issues in v6 of the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Oh and the MRU cluster caching I mentioned here is not the allocation
caching. It's the lookup caching, basically to avoid doing the
xa_load() to look up clusters for consecutive swap operations on the
same vswap cluster (which is the common case with vswap). For v6, it
massively reduces this indirection lookup overhead. Performance-wise
it's an absolute winner, just more complexity (because I need to
handle reference counting carefully).

I also just realized we'll induce the indirection overhead on
allocation here too, even if the cached cluster still have slots for
allocation, because we look up the cluster (which is basically free
for static swap device, but not free for vswap devices). Might need to
take care of that to maintain vswap performance (but it will then
diverge from your existing code...).

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Kairui Song questioned the necessity of handling swap information in both the virtual and physical layers, pointing out that it would introduce complexity and duplication of effort.

Reviewer noted that vswap adds a new layer of complexity and questioned the value of implementing it, citing potential performance issues under heavy memory pressure and the need for additional code such as vswap_can_swapin_thp.

Reviewer Kairui Song suggested moving the per-cpu cache to si level, which would provide a stable cache for allocation paths, and proposed implementing a more explicit per-si fast path to handle plist rotation in a lockless manner.

Reviewer noted that the current implementation of vswap can lead to frequent invalidation of the shared per-cpu cache due to alternating allocation between physical swap and virtual swap under heavy memory pressure, requesting a solution to mitigate this issue.

Reviewer Kairui Song suggested deferring the recording of memory control group (memcg) information until charging and recording, which would simplify the code and reduce the possibility of pinning a memcg.

Reviewer Kairui Song noted that the complexity of virtual swap (vswap) is still needed and suggested saving effort by not making everything dynamic if the ultimate goal is to account for vswap separately.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't really get why we would need to do that? If you put the PFN
info in the virtual / upper layer, then the count info, locking, and
all swap IO synchronization (via folio lock), dup (current protected
by ci lock / folio lock), and allocation (folio_alloc_swap), are all
handled in this layer.

The physical / lower layer will just hold a reverse entry on
folio_realloc_swap, or no entry at all (no physical layer used, zswap,
or after swap allocation but before IO) right?

Looking up the actual folio from the physical layer will be a bit
slower since it needs to resolve the reverse entry, but the only place
we need to do that is things like migrate, compaction (none of them
exist yet) which seems totally fine?

---

Thanks. Not too complicated, actually our internal kernel
implementation still using si->percpu cluster, and use a counter for
the rotation and each order have a counter :P, it's a bit ugly but
works fine. It still serves pretty well just like the global percpu
cluster, YoungJun's previous per ci percpu cluster also still provides
the fast path, many ways to do that.

---

I mean if the per-cpu cache is moved to si level, then whoever enters
the allocation path of a si will almost always get a stable percpu
cache to use, even if the last used si changes.

We might better have a more explicit per si fast path for that. The
plist rotation should better be done in a different (will be even
better if lockless) way.

---

If mixed backend means phys vs zero vs zswap, then we already have
part of that covered with the current swap cache except for the phys
part (zswap part seems very doable with fujunjie's work).
swap_cache_alloc_folio will ensure there is no mixed zerobit, it can
be easily extended to ensure there is no mixed zswap as well
(according to what I've learned from fujunjie's code). Similar logic
for phys detection I think.

---

Maybe you don't need to record memcg here since folio->memcg already
have that info?

I previously had a patch:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260220-swap-table-p4-v1-7-104795d19815@tencent.com/

The defers the recording of memcg, the behavior is almost identical to
before, but charging & recording should be cleaner and you don't need
to record memcg at allocation time hence maybe reduce the possibility
of pinning a memcg. I didn't include that in P4 just to reduce LOC,
maybe can be resent or included.

---

Good to know, it's not too messy to make everything dynamic, but if
our ultimate goal is to account for them separately, maybe we can save
some effort here.

pattern always follows that?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/list_lru: drain before clearing xarray entry on reparent

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/list_lru: drain before clearing xarray entry on reparent
[PATCH v2] mm/list_lru: drain before clearing xarray entry on reparent

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a concurrency issue in the list_lru code by draining the per-node lists before clearing the xarray entry on reparenting. The problem occurs when a concurrent thread sees the xarray entry as NULL and walks to the parent, causing corruption of adjacent items' links. To fix this, the order is reversed: each per-node list is reparented and marked dead (LONG_MIN) before the xarray entry is cleared. This ensures that any concurrent list_lru operation will either take the dying memcg's per-node lock or see LONG_MIN and walk to the parent, where the items now live.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 1/2] mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series proposes to bypass the mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead, which is intended to prevent speculative mmap readahead when a file appears to be a random-access workload. However, this heuristic does not fit well with the VM_EXEC path, as it has its own constraints and is bounded by the VMA. The patch aims to keep the counter accounting symmetric by skipping both increments and decrements for VM_EXEC mappings.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 2/2] mm: use mapping_max_folio_order() for force_thp_readahead order
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series modifies the force_thp_readahead path in do_sync_mmap_readahead() to use mapping_max_folio_order() for VM_HUGEPAGE mappings, allowing them to request the maximum folio order supported by the mapping. This change is particularly relevant on arm64 configurations with 64K base page sizes, where it enables large-folio readahead requests instead of falling back to base-page readahead.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Identified the need for the change and expressed agreement with its implementation. Suggested adding a Reviewed-by tag.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch improves large folio readahead performance for executable memory by simplifying the logic and capping the maximum THP (Transparent Huge Pages) order to 2M. The changes are based on benchmark results that show a significant improvement in cold fault and random access times, with an average reduction of 50% and 23%, respectively. The patch builds upon previous revisions and includes suggestions from multiple contributors to ensure correctness and performance.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the patch series is missing a [0/N] cover letter, which was present in previous revisions and should be retained.

Andrew Morton noted that the patch's logic for deciding THP order was simplified, but he is concerned about the potential impact on memory pressure from large forced readahead when mapping_max_folio_order() is high.
review_comments.raw_body:
